sections.heading: The Science of Vocabulary Acquisition
sections.body:
Research shows you need 12-15 exposures to a new word before it moves to long-term memory. Here's how to optimize:

1. First encounter: see/hear the word in context
2. Second encounter: look up the definition
3. Third encounter: use it in a sentence
4. Encounters 4-8: spaced repetition reviews
5. Encounters 9-12: use in conversation/writing
6. Encounters 13+: word is in your active vocabulary

Passive vocabulary: words you understand when reading/hearing
Active vocabulary: words you can use in speaking/writing

Goal: Move words from passive → active through deliberate practice.

sections.heading: Word Parts — The Key to Unlocking Vocabulary
sections.body:
English borrows from Latin, Greek, and Germanic roots. Learning 50 common roots unlocks 1000+ words:

Latin roots:
• duct/duc = lead: conduct, produce, educate, reduce, introduce
• port = carry: transport, export, import, report, portable
• scrib/script = write: describe, prescribe, subscribe, manuscript
• spect = look: inspect, respect, expect, perspective, spectacle

Greek roots:
• phon = sound: telephone, phonics, symphony, microphone
• graph = write/draw: photograph, biography, autograph, graphic
• auto = self: automatic, autobiography, autopilot, autonomy
• bio = life: biology, biography, biodiversity, antibiotic

Common prefixes:
un- (not): unhappy, unlikely, unusual
re- (again): return, rebuild, reconsider
pre- (before): predict, prevent, preheat
dis- (opposite): disappear, disagree, disconnect
mis- (wrong): misunderstand, mislead, mistake
over- (too much): overwork, overthink, overdue
under- (not enough): underestimate, underpaid, underline

sections.heading: Collocations — Why 'Make a Mistake' Not 'Do a Mistake'
sections.body:
Collocations are word partnerships that sound natural to native speakers. Breaking them sounds wrong even if grammatically correct.

Make vs Do:
make: a decision, a mistake, progress, money, an effort, friends
do: homework, exercise, a job, work, damage, a favor

Common collocations:
• Take a break / take a photo / take responsibility
• Have a look / have a chat / have a problem
• Pay attention / pay a visit / pay the price
• Break a rule / break a record / break the ice
• Run a business / run an errand / run out of

Learning tip: Don't learn single words — learn word partnerships. Store them as chunks: 'make a decision' not just 'decision'.

sections.heading: Practical Vocabulary Building System
sections.body:
Daily routine (15 minutes):
1. Morning: Learn 3 new words with collocations and examples
2. Lunch: Review yesterday's words (2 minutes)
3. Evening: Write 3 sentences using today's words

Weekly:
• Read one article in English (highlight unknown words)
• Watch one English video with subtitles
• Have one conversation using new vocabulary

Tools:
• Anki (free flashcard app with spaced repetition)
• Vocabulary notebooks organized by topic
• Word of the day websites/apps

Track progress: Keep a vocabulary journal with date, word, definition, example sentence, and personal connection.

summary: Vocabulary building is systematic, not random. Learn word families, master common roots and affixes, study collocations, and use spaced repetition. 15 minutes daily beats 2 hours weekly.
